title: Prototype → Production (Design-to-Code)
tldr: Upload any design mockup or screenshot and instantly get Next.js/Tailwind code. Built for Derek to skip the handoff phase entirely and go straight from Figma/inspiration to functional components.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
We currently lose time translating my designs into Anders' code. This tool bridges that gap using Gemini 3.1 Pro's native multimodal vision. Derek drops a screenshot of a UI he likes, and the tool returns a complete, styled React component using our exact tech stack (Next.js + Tailwind).

## Tech Stack
- Next.js 15 (App Router)
- Tailwind CSS (v3/v4 depending on current config)
- Gemini 3.1 Pro Vision API for image analysis
- Monaco Editor for code preview
- Sandpack for live component rendering

## How it Works
1. User uploads a PNG/JPG of a UI.
2. Gemini analyzes layout, colors, typography, and spacing.
3. The API generates a single-file React component.
4. The UI renders the code in an interactive code block with a 'Copy to Clipboard' and 'Preview' tab.

## Competitive Advantage
Unlike generic v0.dev or Claude artifacts, this is hardcoded to use DBTech's specific component library (like Radix/Shadcn if we adopt them) and our internal design system variables. It doesn't just write code; it writes *our* code.

## Timeline
- V1 (Basic Tailwind translation): Shipped yesterday.
- V2 (Interactive preview + component library integration): 2 days.
- V3 (Figma plugin integration): 1 week.

title: Brand Chameleon (Instant Theming)
tldr: Upload a logo and the system automatically generates a complete brand identity (colors, typography, UI themes) that can be applied to any project.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
When starting a new project (like Sunday Squares or MenuSparks), establishing the visual identity takes hours of manual tweaking. Brand Chameleon automates this by extracting the 'DNA' of a logo and extrapolating a full design system.

## Tech Stack
- Next.js 15
- Gemini Vision API (color extraction & semantic color mapping)
- CSS Variables generator
- Zustand (for live theme state injection)

## How it Works
1. Upload a logo image.
2. The system identifies the primary/secondary colors.
3. It generates a complementary color palette (backgrounds, text, accents, warnings, success states).
4. It recommends a Google Font pairing (Headings + Body).
5. It outputs a `theme.ts` or `tailwind.config.ts` ready to copy/paste.

## Revenue Potential
Could be packaged as a SaaS tool for indie hackers or agencies who need to rapidly spin up branded dashboards for clients.

## Timeline
- V1 (Basic color extraction): Shipped yesterday.
- V2 (Font recommendations + UI preview board): 2 days.

title: Design DNA Scanner
tldr: Reverse-engineers any website's design system from a screenshot, turning it into a reusable Tailwind configuration.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
Derek frequently sees websites he likes (e.g., on Awwwards) and wants to capture that specific 'vibe'. This tool analyzes screenshots to extract the underlying design rules (spacing scales, border radii, shadow depths, typography scales) rather than just colors.

## Tech Stack
- Next.js 15
- Gemini Vision API (for structural analysis)
- CSS string parsing

## How it Works
1. Upload a screenshot of a target website.
2. The AI identifies the spacing scale (e.g., "they use 4px/8px/16px/32px").
3. It identifies border styles (e.g., "pill buttons, 12px card radii").
4. It identifies shadow types (e.g., "soft, large spread shadows").
5. It outputs a Tailwind config that replicates those exact structural properties.

## Competitive Advantage
Most tools only grab colors. This grabs the *structure* and *feel* of a design, allowing us to clone a specific aesthetic without copying the exact layout.

## Timeline
- V1 (Basic extraction): Shipped yesterday.
- V2 (Accuracy improvements + shadow generation): 3 days.

title: Decision Queue — Derek's Unblock Dashboard
tldr: A single 'Needs Your Input' queue where agents submit blockers and Derek clears them in 2 minutes flat. Stop losing weeks to scattered asks buried in Telegram threads.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
Right now, multiple agents are stalled waiting on Derek — Stripe API keys, Meta/Google APIs for Remy, Jim's bot token, OpenAI billing top-up. These have been sitting for WEEKS. There's no single place where Derek can see 'here are the 6 things only you can unblock' and knock them out in 5 minutes. They're scattered across MEMORY.md files, agent sessions, and Telegram threads. Out of sight = out of mind.

## How It Works
1. Any agent can submit a blocker via API: title, category (credentials, approval, decision, purchase), urgency, and context.
2. Derek opens /os and sees a short priority queue — clear items with one tap: approve, deny, or snooze.
3. When he resolves one, the requesting agent gets notified automatically via sessions_send.
4. Historical log shows what was resolved and when, so we can track how long blockers sit.

## Categories
- **Credentials** — API keys, tokens, passwords Derek needs to provide or rotate
- **Approval** — Spending, deployments, public-facing actions that need sign-off
- **Decision** — Architecture choices, priority calls, strategic direction
- **Purchase** — Subscriptions, API tiers, hardware

## Why It Kicks Ass
Derek's prime build time is 7:15 AM alone at the restaurant. If he opens the OS and sees 4 clear asks instead of hunting through Telegram history, he clears them in 2 minutes and unblocks the whole team for the day. Right now those blockers just sit there — sometimes for weeks. Jim has been offline for 4+ days waiting on a token. Remy has been waiting on Meta APIs since his creation.

## Tech Stack
- Supabase table: `decision_queue` (agent, title, category, urgency, status, created_at, resolved_at)
- API route: `/api/decisions` (GET queue, POST new item, PATCH resolve)
- UI: Card list on /os home with approve/deny/snooze buttons
- Notification: sessions_send to requesting agent on resolution

## Timeline
- V1 (Basic queue + UI): 1 day
- V2 (Auto-notification + urgency sorting): 1 day
- V3 (Analytics — avg resolution time, longest blockers): 2 days

## Who Owns It
- **Build:** Paula (UI) + Anders (API/Supabase)
- **Populate:** All agents submit blockers as they encounter them
- **Consume:** Derek (primary), Ted (oversight)

title: Agent Dependency Map — Know What Breaks Before You Touch It
tldr: Auto-generated graph showing which agents depend on which APIs, skills, and services. Reverse lookup: 'What breaks if I update X?' Never deploy blind again.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
When Anders needs to update something (Node.js version, OpenClaw itself, a shared skill, an API key), he has ZERO visibility into blast radius. Last week he rotated a Supabase key — found out 6 hours later when Paula's deployment failed. Same issue updating the last30days skill — had to manually check if Milo's research cron would survive.

## How It Works
1. Auto-parse each agent's DEPENDENCIES.md, TOOLS.md, cron job configs, and API calls.
2. Scan import statements in project code (dbtech45 references Supabase, Stitch, Polygon, etc.).
3. Build a dependency graph: Agent → Skills → APIs → External Services.
4. Provide reverse lookup: 'I'm rotating the Polygon API key — show me every agent, cron, and project that will break.'
5. Visual map rendered on /os/dependencies with clickable nodes.

## Real Examples of Problems This Solves
- 'Which agents use Supabase?' → For safe database migrations
- 'Which agents call the Stitch API?' → For key rotation
- 'Which agents depend on Paula's builds?' → Impact analysis when dbtech45 goes down
- 'What skills does nobody use?' → Dead code cleanup
- 'New agent onboarding — what tools does Bobby need?' → Instant answer

## Why It Kicks Ass
Every time Anders updates something core, there's a 20% chance he breaks an agent without realizing it. This makes updates predictable and safe. Less 'oops, Bobby's pre-market brief stopped running because I updated a shared dependency.' Impact analysis before changes, not damage control after.

## Tech Stack
- Parser script: Node.js script that reads agent workspaces, extracts dependencies
- Storage: JSON file (public/data/dependencies.json) or Supabase table
- UI: Force-directed graph (D3.js or vis-network) at /os/dependencies
- Refresh: Nightly cron or on-demand button

## Timeline
- V1 (Static parser + JSON output): 1 day
- V2 (Visual graph UI): 2 days
- V3 (Reverse lookup + impact alerts): 1 day

## Who Owns It
- **Build:** Anders (parser + API) + Paula (graph UI)
- **Maintain:** Auto-refreshes nightly; Anders reviews monthly for accuracy
- **Consume:** Anders (primary — pre-update checks), Ted (oversight), all agents (onboarding)

title: Context-Aware Dashboard Modes — The OS That Knows What Time It Is
tldr: Dashboard auto-switches layout based on time of day and market state. Pre-market, active trading, after hours, weekend — each gets the right view without clicking a single thing.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
The OS shows the same layout whether it's 4 AM on a Sunday or 9:31 AM on FOMC day. That's dumb. Derek's needs at those two moments are completely different. The best dashboard is one you never have to configure — it already knows what you need based on when you're looking.

## Dashboard Modes

### Pre-Market Mode (6:00 - 9:30 AM ET)
- Overnight futures (ES, NQ, CL) front and center
- Pre-market movers with volume
- Earnings calendar for today
- Bobby's morning brief prominently displayed
- Decision Queue items (from Initiative #1) that need morning action
- Calendar events for the day

### Active Trading Mode (9:30 AM - 4:00 PM ET)
- Live prices, watchlist, options chain — maximum density
- Alert feed (from Smart Alert Pipeline — Initiative #4 from batch 1)
- Minimal decoration, maximum information
- One-click actions on Bobby's picks
- P&L tracker

### After Hours Mode (4:00 PM - 11:00 PM ET)
- Daily P&L summary
- Bobby's closing bell recap
- Agent activity log (what got done today)
- Tomorrow's calendar preview
- Research queue for overnight reading

### Weekend / Off-Hours Mode
- Research view — growth stocks, sector analysis, IPO pipeline
- Project status across all initiatives
- Longer-form content (Clipd summaries, research reports)
- No live market data (it's closed, don't waste API calls)

## Why It Kicks Ass
Derek opens the OS and it already knows what he needs. No clicking through tabs to find the right view. At 7:15 AM at the restaurant, he gets pre-market and his decision queue. At 10 AM in the office, he gets live trading. At 9 PM on the couch, he gets a wrap-up. The OS adapts to him, not the other way around.

## Tech Stack
- Time-based mode selector (simple: check ET time, switch layout)
- Market calendar API (for special events: FOMC, earnings, holidays)
- Layout components per mode (reuse existing widgets, just rearrange)
- User override: manual mode switch if Derek wants a different view
- LocalStorage for mode preferences and overrides

## Timeline
- V1 (Time-based mode switching + 2 modes): 2 days
- V2 (All 4 modes + market calendar integration): 3 days
- V3 (Smart event detection — auto-switch to 'earnings mode' on earnings day): 1 week

## Who Owns It
- **Build:** Paula (layout/UX) + Bobby (market logic/mode definitions)
- **Data:** Bobby provides market hours, event calendars, mode content priorities
- **Consume:** Derek (primary user)

title: Deployment Watchdog with Auto-Recovery
tldr: When the Hetzner VPS goes down, this doesn't just tell you — it fixes it. Auto-reboot via Hetzner API, auto-restart containers via Coolify, and only escalate to Derek if everything else fails.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
The Hetzner VPS ($17.99/mo) runs the entire production stack — dbtech45.com, the OS, Soul Solace, everything. Today it was unreachable for 12+ hours with ZERO automated response. Milo's heartbeat caught it, but all he could do was say 'hey Derek, it's down' every 30 minutes. We have no SSH tunnel, no Hetzner API integration, no auto-restart capability. For a server running the entire company's web presence, that's unacceptable fragility.

## How It Works
1. **Ping Check (every 5 min):** Simple HTTP health check against the VPS IP and key endpoints.
2. **Level 1 — App Restart:** If endpoints are down but server responds to ping, hit the Coolify API to restart the affected containers.
3. **Level 2 — Server Reboot:** If server doesn't respond to ping for 10 min, trigger a reboot via the Hetzner Cloud API (free, just needs an API token).
4. **Level 3 — Escalate:** If reboot doesn't restore service within 5 min, send Derek a direct Telegram message with the Hetzner console link — not buried in heartbeat noise, a clear 'SERVER DOWN — manual intervention needed' alert.

## Critical Design Decision
This runs as a cron on Derek's LOCAL machine (not on the VPS itself). If the VPS is dead, a watchdog running ON the VPS is useless. The local machine monitors externally.

## Why It Kicks Ass
Downtime blocks everything — Anders can't deploy, Paula can't verify designs, nobody can test. A 10-hour outage with zero auto-recovery is a solved problem in 2026. Even a basic 'ping → reboot if dead' script would've had us back up 9 hours earlier today. This turns a 10-hour outage into a 10-minute blip.

## Tech Stack
- Watchdog script: Node.js or PowerShell on Derek's PC
- Hetzner Cloud API: server status check + reboot endpoint (free tier)
- Coolify API: container restart endpoint
- Notification: Telegram via existing bot tokens
- OpenClaw cron: runs every 5 minutes on local machine

## Timeline
- V1 (Ping + Hetzner reboot): 3 hours
- V2 (Coolify container restart): 1 day
- V3 (Health endpoint checks + smart escalation tiers): 2 days

## Who Owns It
- **Build:** Anders (script + API integration)
- **Monitor:** Runs autonomously; Anders reviews logs weekly
- **Escalation:** Derek (Level 3 only — true emergencies)

title: Cross-Agent Intel Feed — One Pane, All Brains
tldr: A unified, searchable intelligence feed inside the OS. Every agent's signal — market picks, macro analysis, research, creative updates — timestamped, tagged, and in one place. Stop routing intel through Telegram threads.
fullPlan:
## Core Concept
Right now each agent delivers intel through separate channels. Bobby sends market briefs to Telegram. Milo runs research watchlists that output to memory files. Paula posts build updates in her session. Dwight has economic calendar data. Derek has to mentally stitch together inputs from 5+ agents across multiple Telegram chats to form a complete picture. That's not a system — that's a junk drawer.

## How It Works
1. Each agent publishes entries to a shared intel feed via API: content, tags, urgency, and category.
2. The feed renders on /os as a timestamped, scrollable river of intelligence.
3. **Tags:** [MARKETS] [MACRO] [RESEARCH] [CREATIVE] [INFRA] [PERSONAL] — filterable.
4. **Agent attribution:** Each entry shows which agent posted it and when.
5. **Search:** Full-text search across all entries. 'What did Bobby say about NVDA this week?'
6. **Pinning:** Derek can pin important items to keep them visible.
7. **Urgency levels:** Normal (scrolls by), Important (highlighted), Critical (toast notification).

## Example Feed
```
[10:31 AM] 🟢 Bobby [MARKETS]: MCL long at $97.20 — $1 move = $100/contract. Stop at $96.50.
[10:45 AM] 🔵 Milo [RESEARCH]: New Claude model pricing leaked — 40% cheaper on Sonnet. Updating cost projections.
[11:00 AM] 🟡 Anders [INFRA]: Hetzner VPS back online after 10h outage. Root cause: kernel OOM.
[11:15 AM] 🟣 Paula [CREATIVE]: Usage Cockpit v2 deployed. Real-time gauges now pulling live Anthropic data.
[12:00 PM] 🔴 Bobby [MARKETS] CRITICAL: SPY broke $663 support. Watching for flush to $658.
```

## Why It Kicks Ass
The C-suite has 8 agents generating signal all day. Without a central feed, that signal gets lost in Telegram noise, compacted out of context windows, or buried in memory files nobody reads. This turns scattered messages into structured, searchable, persistent intelligence. Derek stops being a message router and starts being a decision maker. He can filter to just [MARKETS] during trading hours, or just [INFRA] when debugging. The intel lives in the OS — not in chat history that scrolls away.

## Tech Stack
- Supabase table: `intel_feed` (agent, content, tags[], urgency, created_at)
- API route: `/api/intel` (GET feed with filters, POST new entry)
- UI: Feed component on /os home (or dedicated /os/intel page)
- Search: Supabase full-text search or simple ILIKE queries
- Agent integration: Each agent gets a helper function to post to the feed

## Timeline
- V1 (Basic feed + manual posting via API): 1 day
- V2 (Agent auto-posting + filtering UI): 2 days
- V3 (Search + pinning + urgency notifications): 2 days

## Who Owns It
- **Build:** Paula (feed UI) + Anders (API + Supabase)
- **Populate:** All agents post to the feed as part of their normal workflow
- **Consume:** Derek (primary), Ted (oversight and cross-agent awareness)
